title: The Haitian Constitution That Freed All
event_summary: Just over a year after independence, Haiti's Secretary of State Juste Chanlatte drafted and Emperor Jean-Jacques Dessalines promulgated the 1805 Imperial Constitution, which contained a provision unprecedented in world history: Article 14 declared that all citizens of Haiti, regardless of skin color, would legally be considered 'Black.' This single clause abolished racial classification as a legal category, making Haiti the first nation on Earth to constitutionally eliminate racial hierarchy and declare universal civic equality across color lines.
why_forgotten: Western powers — particularly France, Britain, and the United States — deliberately isolated Haiti diplomatically and economically for decades, terrified that its radical racial egalitarianism would inspire enslaved populations elsewhere. Haiti's constitutional innovations were excluded from the canon of Enlightenment political philosophy, and historians of constitutionalism long centered only the French and American revolutionary documents. The crushing debt France extorted from Haiti in 1825 further ensured the nation's marginalization from global narratives of progress.
why_it_matters: Haiti's 1805 constitution was the world's first legal instrument to abolish race as a state category, predating the 14th Amendment to the U.S. Constitution by over sixty years and anti-apartheid constitutions by nearly two centuries. Its radical framework influenced Latin American independence movements, particularly Simón Bolívar, who received military aid and asylum from Haiti on the condition he abolish slavery. Modern debates about colorblind constitutionalism, reparations, and structural racism trace back to questions this document posed first.
